title: The Grief That Comes with Healing
researcherTopic: sensorimotor psychotherapy, the body in trauma treatment
metaDescription: The grief that comes with healing complex trauma is a profound loss. Learn how to navigate this difficult but necessary part of the journey back to yourself.
imageDescription: A delicate porcelain bowl, shattered and then repaired with shimmering gold, sits on a rustic wooden table. Warm, gentle morning light streams in from a nearby window, illuminating the golden cracks and casting a soft glow on the scene. The image evokes a sense of beauty in imperfection and the healing power of repair.
htmlContent:
Healing is not an arrival. It is a constant act of grieving for the person you had to be to survive.

The Unacknowledged Loss of Self

We talk about healing as if it is a destination, a clean shore we wash up on after a long and harrowing journey through the storm of the past. But for those of us who have navigated the treacherous waters of complex trauma, the reality is far more nuanced, far more layered. The work of healing is often, at its core, a work of grieving. It is the slow, painful, and profoundly necessary process of mourning the childhood we never had, the safety we never felt, and the self that was lost in the chaos of survival. This is not a one-time event; it is a continuous unfolding, a constant companion on the path of return. We are not just grieving the things that happened; we are grieving the things that didn’t happen—the secure attachment, the spontaneous joy, the unwavering belief in our own worth. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen countless individuals grapple with this profound sense of loss, a grief so deep and pervasive it can feel like a fundamental part of their being.

This loss extends beyond the emotional landscape into the very structure of memory and identity. For many with a history of complex trauma, there is no coherent narrative of the past, but rather a series of fragmented images, bodily sensations, and unexplained emotional states. It’s the grief of not having a story that makes sense, of feeling like a stranger in the narrative of your own life. This fragmentation is not a personal failing but a brilliant adaptive strategy of a nervous system trying to protect itself from overwhelm. Yet, the cost is a persistent sense of unreality, a feeling of being disconnected from one’s own history, which itself becomes a source of deep, often unspoken, grief. We mourn the solid ground of a known past, a foundation upon which a stable sense of self could have been built.

The nervous system doesn’t respond to what you believe. It responds to what it senses.

This is not a grief that can be reasoned with or rationalized away. It lives in the body, in the tight knot in the stomach, the ache in the chest, the constant, low-level hum of anxiety. It is the body’s memory of a world that was not safe, a world where vigilance was the only currency that mattered. To heal, we must learn to turn toward this bodily felt sense of loss, to meet it with a quality of attention that is both gentle and unwavering. We must, as the pioneering work of Stephen Porges has shown us, learn the language of our own nervous system, to understand its signals not as pathologies to be fixed but as communications to be heard. It requires that we bypass the thinking mind and drop into the raw data of sensation, for it is here, in the pre-verbal, pre-cognitive space of the body, that the real story of the past is held and where the potential for a new future can be found.

The Architecture of Survival

The person you became to survive was a masterpiece of adaptation. Every defense mechanism, every emotional wall, every pattern of relating was a brilliant, life-saving strategy in the face of overwhelming circumstances. To begin to heal is to begin to dismantle this architecture of survival, and that dismantling is, itself, a form of loss. We grieve the loss of the familiar, even when the familiar was painful. We grieve the loss of the identity we constructed, even when that identity was a cage. A client once described this as feeling like they were standing in the rubble of their own life, with no blueprint for what to build next. It is a disorienting and terrifying place to be, a liminal space between the no longer and the not yet.

These survival patterns, once so essential, become prisons in adulthood. The hypervigilance that kept you safe as a child now prevents you from experiencing intimacy and connection. The emotional numbing that allowed you to endure the unendurable now robs you of joy and vitality. The fawn response that appeased the volatile adults in your life now leads to a chronic pattern of self-abandonment. Grieving this architecture means acknowledging both its necessity in the past and its cost in the present. It’s a complex sorrow, tinged with a strange sense of gratitude for the parts of you that did whatever it took to get you through. It requires a profound act of self-compassion to honor these survival strategies even as you begin the painstaking work of letting them go.

What matters here: the work of researchers like Pat Ogden becomes so vital. Her development of sensorimotor psychotherapy offers a powerful roadmap for navigating this territory, for working directly with the body’s wisdom to release the trauma that is stored there. It is not about talking about what happened, but about experiencing, in a safe and titrated way, the body’s response to what happened. It is about completing the thwarted impulses of fight and flight, of reaching out and pushing away, that were frozen in time by the trauma. It is a process of learning to trust the body’s intelligence, to follow its lead as it guides us toward integration and wholeness. We learn to notice the subtle shifts in sensation, the flicker of an impulse, the deep sigh of release. We learn that the body has a story to tell, a story that is often far more honest and complete than the one our minds have constructed. And in the telling of that story, in the witnessing of its unfolding, we begin to find our way back to ourselves.

The Myth of Moving On

Our culture is obsessed with the idea of “moving on,” of leaving the past behind and striding confidently into the future. But for those who carry the weight of complex trauma, this is a cruel and impossible demand. There is no moving on from something that is still living inside of you. There is only moving with, moving through, moving in a way that honors the depth and complexity of your experience. The grief that comes with healing is not a sign that you are stuck or broken; it is a sign that you are finally, bravely, turning toward the truth of what you have endured. It is a testament to your resilience, to your capacity to feel, and to your unwavering commitment to your own liberation.

The societal pressure to be “over it” is a form of gaslighting that can be incredibly damaging. It invalidates the lived reality of trauma and reinforces the shame that so many survivors already carry. This pressure to perform wellness, to present a tidy narrative of recovery, forces us to betray our own experience. The truth is that healing is messy, non-linear, and often involves periods of regression and despair. To grieve is to defy this cultural mandate, to claim your right to your own timeline, your own process. It is a radical act of self-advocacy to say, “Which means I am, and this is what is true for me right now,” without apology or explanation.

The breath doesn’t need your management. It needs your companionship.

We are not trying to get rid of the grief, but to make room for it, to allow it to move through us without overwhelming us. This is the delicate art of pendulation, of moving between the pain of the past and the resource of the present moment. We dip a toe into the waters of grief, and then we return to the solid ground of our current safety. We touch the edges of our sorrow, and then we anchor ourselves in the sensation of our feet on the floor, the feeling of the sun on our skin, the sound of our own breath. This is not a linear process; it is a spiral, a dance, a constant negotiation between the then and the now. And in this dance, we begin to discover that we are larger than our pain, that we can hold both the sorrow and the joy, the grief and the gratitude, the loss and the love. We learn to find the resource in the present moment, as taught in The Body Keeps the Score, Revisited.

The Landscape of the Heart

As we allow ourselves to grieve, the landscape of our hearts begins to change. The barren, desolate places begin to soften. The frozen rivers of emotion begin to thaw. We discover a new capacity for tenderness, for compassion, for a love that is not conditional on being perfect or whole. This is the love that Kalesh speaks of in his work, a love that is not a sentiment but a state of being, a fundamental recognition of our own inherent worth. It is a love that can hold all of our broken pieces, all of our contradictions, all of our messy, beautiful humanity. It is a love that says, “You are welcome here, exactly as you are.”

This is not a passive process. It is an active and ongoing practice of turning toward ourselves with kindness and curiosity. It is the practice of meeting our own pain with the same compassion we would offer to a dear friend. It is the practice of forgiving ourselves for the ways we had to survive, for the choices we made when we had no other options. It is the practice of celebrating our own resilience, of honoring the strength and courage it has taken to get this far. And as we do this, as we cultivate this inner landscape of love and acceptance, we begin to find that the grief is not the end of the story. It is the beginning of a new one, a story of a heart that has been broken open, a story of a self that is being reborn. A story that is explored in more detail in Kalesh's teachings on consciousness.

The Eloquence of Tears

There will be tears. There will be moments when the grief feels like a tidal wave, threatening to pull you under. And in those moments, the most radical act of self-love is to let the tears come. To allow the body to speak its truth in the eloquent language of sorrow. Each tear is a prayer, a release, a letting go of a burden you have carried for far too long. Each sob is a song of liberation, a reclaiming of the parts of yourself that have been silenced and shamed. This is not a sign of weakness; it is a sign of profound strength, of a courage that is willing to feel it all.

The body has a grammar. Most of us never learned to read it.

We have been taught to fear our own emotions, to see them as problems to be solved or enemies to be vanquished. But what if our emotions are not the enemy? What if they are simply energy in motion, messengers from the deep, asking for our attention, our presence, our compassionate witness? What if the grief is not something to be overcome, but something to be honored, to be held, to be allowed to move through us in its own time and in its own way? This is the invitation of healing, the call to come home to the full spectrum of our human experience. It is a journey from the head to the heart, from the story to the sensation, from the past to the present moment. And it is a journey that is worth taking, for on the other side of the grief lies a love that is vaster and more unconditional than anything we could have ever imagined. It is a journey of understanding our own Four F Responses.

The Sacred Work of Integration

Ultimately, the grief that comes with healing is not about what we have lost, but about what we are reclaiming. We are reclaiming our right to feel, our right to be messy and imperfect, our right to take up space in the world. We are reclaiming our bodies, our voices, our stories. We are reclaiming the parts of ourselves that we had to disown in order to survive. This is the sacred work of integration, of bringing all the disparate pieces of ourselves back into a cohesive and coherent whole. It is the work of a lifetime, a journey without a final destination, a path that unfolds with each step we take.

This integration is not a quiet, contemplative process that happens in isolation. It is forged in the crucible of relationship—with ourselves, with others, and with the world. As we learn to offer ourselves the compassion and acceptance we never received, we become capable of receiving it from others. As we learn to trust our own inner experience, we become more discerning about who we allow into our lives. We begin to gravitate toward people who can meet us in our wholeness, who are not afraid of our scars, who can celebrate our resilience. This is the relational healing that is at the heart of recovery from complex trauma. It is the slow, steady work of building a new nervous system, one that is wired for connection, not just survival.

The most important things in life cannot be understood — only experienced.

And on this path, we discover that we are not alone. We are walking with all those who have come before us, all those who have navigated this same territory of loss and renewal. We are walking with the wisdom of the ages, the teachings of the mystics and the sages, the insights of the poets and the artists. And we are walking with each other, in a shared field of courage and compassion, of vulnerability and strength. We are, together, rewriting the story of trauma, not as a story of brokenness, but as a story of resilience, of transformation, of the unbreakable spirit of the human heart. A story that is further explored in Understanding Emotional Flashbacks.

The real question is not whether this applies to you. You already know it does. The real question is what you are going to do with that knowledge now that you cannot un-know it.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does healing have to involve so much grief?

Grief is the natural and necessary response to loss, and healing from complex trauma involves acknowledging and processing a multitude of losses. We grieve the loss of a safe and nurturing childhood, the loss of our sense of self, and the loss of the life we might have had if the trauma had not occurred. This grief is not a detour on the path of healing; it is the path itself. It is the emotional expression of coming to terms with reality, and it is through this process that we can begin to integrate the past and create a new future.

What if I feel like the grief will never end?

It is a common and understandable fear that the grief will be overwhelming and endless. However, the process of grieving is not a linear one, and it does not last forever. With the right support and resources, we can learn to move through the waves of grief without being consumed by them, and we can discover that on the other side of the pain lies a renewed sense of peace and wholeness. The intensity of the grief will lessen over time, and it will be interspersed with periods of joy, connection, and gratitude. The goal is not to eliminate grief, but to expand our capacity to hold it alongside all the other emotions of a full life.

How can I support myself through the grieving process?

Supporting yourself through the grieving process involves a combination of self-compassion, somatic awareness, and connection with others. Practices like mindfulness, gentle movement, and spending time in nature can help to regulate the nervous system and create a sense of safety in the body. Connecting with a therapist, a support group, or trusted friends can provide the relational support that is so essential for healing. It is also important to be patient with yourself, to allow the process to unfold at its own pace, and to celebrate the small victories along the way. Remember that you are not broken, and you do not need to be fixed. You are simply a human being who has endured a great deal and is now finding your way home.

There is a tenderness that is earned through the courageous act of turning toward our own pain. It is a tenderness that is forged in the fires of grief, a tenderness that is born of a heart that has been broken open and made more spacious, more compassionate, more alive. This is the gift of the journey, the unexpected grace that meets us in the depths of our sorrow. It is the discovery that even in our most broken places, we are still and always whole.

Are you willing to be uncomfortable long enough for something to shift?
